title: Chapter 6: The Contraband Economy
content: By the end of week one, Chloe had turned Ward B into a marketplace.
The birdbath economy was thriving.

Denise traded a tube of expensive lip balm for a bag of rice crackers. Sarah swapped her romance novel for three tea bags and a packet of instant oatmeal. An anonymous patient left a working USB charger in exchange for a handwritten note that said simply: "You are not alone."
Chloe sat on the courtyard bench and watched the transactions happen. Furtive. Quick. The speed of people who had learned that anything visible could be taken away.
"You have created a black market," Mia said, sitting down beside her.
"I have created a community. The market is just how they talk to each other."
"Nurse Cross is going to notice."

"She already has." Chloe nodded toward the window where Nurse Cross stood, watching the courtyard. "But she cannot shut it down without admitting it exists. And she cannot admit it exists without admitting she lost control."
"So she is stuck."
"Exactly."
That afternoon, Chloe smuggled in the router.

It was not elegant. Derek the orderly had brought it in exchange for a pack of cigarettes she had traded Denise's lip balm for. She had never smoked in her life. She felt slightly ridiculous holding the cigarette pack.
But the router was real. A tiny portable travel router, small enough to fit in her palm.
She handed it to Mia at dinner, sliding it across the table under a napkin.
"Can you make this work?"
Mia examined it. "It is a TP-Link. Default firmware. I can have it running in ten minutes."
"Where will you hide it?"
"The ceiling panel in the bathroom. There is a junction box with a live outlet. No one ever checks it."
"How do you know that?"
"Because I checked it."
Chloe smiled. "You are terrifying."
"I know."
At dinner, Tiffany ate.
It was not much — a bowl of soup, half a sandwich, a small cup of fruit. But she ate it. Slowly. Deliberately. Like someone rediscovering a skill they thought they had lost.
Chloe watched from across the table and said nothing. Drawing attention to it would break the spell.
But when Tiffany finished the soup and reached for the sandwich, Chloe caught her eye and gave a small nod.
Tiffany nodded back.
It was the first time Chloe had seen her smile.
That night, Mia activated the router.

Chloe sat in the bathroom stall with the lights off, watching the small device blink green in the dark. Mia had connected it to the facility's internal network through the junction box. It was not internet — not yet. But it was a network. Their own network.
"I am calling it the Peacenet Backdoor," Mia whispered through the stall door.
"That is a terrible name."
"It is an accurate name."
"Same thing."

Mia's fingers moved across her tablet. "I am routing the admin network through our router. In twenty-four hours, I will have a map of every connected device in this building. Every camera. Every door lock. Every printer."
"Can you unlock doors with it?"
"Not yet. But I will."
Chloe leaned back against the stall wall and looked at the blinking green light.
A small rebellion. An invisible one.
But it was growing.
The next morning, Chloe found a new item in the birdbath.
A key.
It was small, silver, unmarked. Tied to a piece of string.
There was no note.
Chloe picked it up and turned it over in her hands. It looked like a cabinet key. Or a lockbox key. She had no idea what it opened.
But someone wanted her to have it.
She slipped it into her pocket and kept walking.
The game was getting interesting.
Three days into the contraband economy, Nurse Cross called a ward meeting.
She stood at the front of the common room, clipboard in hand, face arranged into an expression of calm authority.
"There has been a rise in prohibited items circulating among patients," she said. "This is a safety concern. Starting today, random room inspections will be conducted daily."
The room was silent.
Chloe raised her hand.
"Yes, Chloe?"
"Define 'prohibited items.'"
"Anything not issued by the facility."
"So if I trade a recipe for chocolate cake, that is a prohibited item?"
Nurse Cross's eyes narrowed by a fraction. "Any item that has not been logged and approved."
"So the problem is the paperwork, not the items."
A few patients hid smiles.
"The problem is untreated patients making decisions their caregivers should make."
"Ah." Chloe nodded slowly. "So the problem is us making choices. Got it."
The smile in Nurse Cross's eyes was gone now. "You are on thin ice, Chloe."
"Good thing I learned to swim."
After the meeting, Chloe found Mia in the bathroom, hunched over the tablet, the router blinking green from the ceiling panel.
"The network is mapped," Mia said. "Every device. Every IP. Every open port. Including a port I should not be able to see."
"What does that mean?"
"It means there is a computer on this network that is not listed on any floor plan. A hidden server. And it is transmitting data to an external IP every night at 2:00 AM."
"What kind of data?"
"I do not know yet. But it is a lot of it."
Chloe looked at the blinking router. "Can you intercept it?"
"I can try. But if the server detects me, it will log the intrusion. And someone will know we are here."
"Do it anyway."
Mia looked at her. "You understand what you are asking?"

"I understand that we did not ask to be here. We did not sign up for this. We were brought here against our will by people who profit from our captivity. Whatever is on that server, it is theirs. And they owe it to us."
Mia held her gaze for a long moment. Then she turned back to the tablet.
"I am in."
That night, Chloe could not sleep.
She lay in her Yellow Tier room and stared at the ceiling. The window was open a crack — the first night air she had felt in a week. Cold. Clean. Real.
She thought about the hidden server. The external IP. The data leaving Serenity Peak every night at 2:00 AM.
Someone was sending information out. Were they tracking patients? Selling data? Communicating with the families who had paid to send their daughters here?
The questions circled like sharks.
She reached under her pillow and touched the key. Still there. She still did not know what it opened.
But she would find out.
At 2:00 AM, Chloe crept to the bathroom.
Mia was already there, tablet glowing, the router's green light steady.
"It is transmitting now," Mia whispered. "I am piggybacking the connection. Tracing the route."
"Can they see us?"
"Not if this works."
They waited in the dark. The seconds stretched.
Then Mia whispered: "Got it."
"Got what?"
"The destination. The server is sending to a law firm. In New York."
"Which firm?"
Mia turned the tablet.
Chloe read the name and felt the world tilt.
Hart, Chen & Associates.
Her father's firm.
She had been inside Serenity Peak for one week. And every night, while she slept, the facility was sending data to her father's office.
She was not a patient.
She was inventory.
